title: Preposition Mistakes - 50 Mistakes
definition: Prepositions (in, on, at, to, for, with...) are some of the hardest words in English because they don't follow one single logical rule - many verbs and adjectives simply 'pair' with a fixed preposition that you have to learn by heart. Here are 50 of the most common preposition mistakes, explained simply, with the correct fixed pairing to remember.

commonMistakes.why: In English, the adjective 'good' always pairs with the preposition 'at' when talking about a skill or subject - this is simply a fixed combination you need to memorize, not something you can work out logically. Whenever you talk about being skilled at something, reach for 'good at', never 'good in'.

The verb 'reach' already means 'arrive at', so it goes directly before the place with no preposition needed at all. Adding 'to' after 'reach' is one of the most common mistakes among learners, so it's worth practicing the verb without any preposition.
'Discuss' already carries the meaning of 'talk about', so adding the word 'about' afterward is unnecessary repetition. Think of 'discuss' as a verb that swallows 'about' inside its own meaning.
The fixed expression in English is 'married to' someone, not 'married with' - this is simply how native speakers always say it. Practice the phrase as one unit: 'married to', so it becomes automatic.
When you're angry AT a person, English uses 'angry with' (or 'angry at'); 'angry on' is not correct in any context. Use 'angry about' instead when you're upset about a situation rather than a person.

The fixed pairing is 'afraid of', describing what causes the fear - 'afraid from' doesn't exist in standard English. If you're learning this for the first time, just remember: fear is always 'of' something.

'Interested' always pairs with 'in', showing what topic draws your attention - 'interested about' is a common but incorrect substitution. A simple memory aid: your interest goes 'in' something, like stepping into a topic.
The verb 'depend' is always followed by 'on' to show who or what you rely on. This is one of the easiest fixed pairs to memorize since 'depend on' rhymes nicely and sticks in memory.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is capable to do this.
commonMistakes.right: He is capable of doing this.
commonMistakes.why: 'Capable' pairs with 'of', and it must be followed by the -ing form of the verb (doing), not the plain infinitive (to do). Remember the full pattern together: 'capable of' + verb-ing.

commonMistakes.wrong: I congratulate you for your success.
commonMistakes.right: I congratulate you on your success.
commonMistakes.why: The verb 'congratulate' pairs with 'on', not 'for', when mentioning the achievement being celebrated. Practice the set phrase 'congratulate someone on something' as a whole unit.

When you want to point out both similarities and differences between two things, 'compare with' is the safer, more standard choice ('compare to' is used specifically when highlighting a resemblance, like comparing a rose to love). For most everyday comparisons, 'with' is the reliable option.

commonMistakes.wrong: He apologized for his mistake to me.
commonMistakes.right: He apologized to me for his mistake.
commonMistakes.why: The natural word order in English is: apologize + TO the person + FOR the reason. Keeping this order sounds much more natural to native speakers, even though your original meaning was already clear.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am proud about my daughter.
commonMistakes.right: I am proud of my daughter.
commonMistakes.why: 'Proud' always pairs with 'of' to show what or who you feel proud about. This is a very common fixed pairing, so practice saying 'proud of' as one single phrase.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is different than her sister.
commonMistakes.right: She is different from her sister.
commonMistakes.why: In careful, formal English, 'different from' is the standard and universally accepted form ('different than' is common in casual American English, but 'different from' is safer in writing and exams). When in doubt, choose 'different from'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I will explain you the process.
commonMistakes.right: I will explain the process to you.
commonMistakes.why: The verb 'explain' cannot be followed directly by a person - you must add 'to' before the person receiving the explanation. Remember the pattern: explain something TO someone, never explain someone something.

Unlike most comparative adjectives that use 'than' (taller than, faster than), 'senior' and 'junior' are exceptions that pair with 'to'. These two words are worth memorizing separately since they break the usual comparison pattern.

commonMistakes.wrong: I graduated in college last year.
commonMistakes.right: I graduated from college last year.
commonMistakes.why: The fixed expression is 'graduate from' an institution, showing where you completed your studies. Picture yourself moving 'from' the school out into the world - that image can help the preposition stick.

commonMistakes.wrong: She was absent for the meeting.
commonMistakes.right: She was absent from the meeting.
commonMistakes.why: 'Absent' pairs with 'from', showing the place or event someone was missing from. This is a simple fixed pairing worth memorizing directly: absent FROM, not absent FOR.
The verb 'insist' is always followed by 'on' plus the -ing form when describing what someone firmly wants to do. Practice the full pattern: insist ON doing something.
'Wait' pairs with 'for' to show who or what you are expecting. This is one of the most basic and frequently used fixed prepositions in English, so it's worth mastering early.

commonMistakes.wrong: She succeeded to pass the exam.
commonMistakes.right: She succeeded in passing the exam.
commonMistakes.why: 'Succeed' is followed by 'in' plus the -ing form of the verb, not 'to' plus the base verb. Memorize the whole phrase together: succeed IN doing something.
Just like 'good at English' earlier, any subject or skill pairs with 'at', never 'with' or 'in'. Build one simple rule in your mind: good AT + [skill or subject].

commonMistakes.wrong: I will arrive to the airport at 6.
commonMistakes.right: I will arrive at the airport at 6.
commonMistakes.why: 'Arrive' pairs with 'at' for smaller, specific locations like airports, stations, and buildings. Compare this with the next mistake, where cities and countries use a different preposition.

commonMistakes.wrong: We will arrive at London tomorrow.
commonMistakes.right: We will arrive in London tomorrow.
commonMistakes.why: For larger places like cities and countries, 'arrive' pairs with 'in' instead of 'at'. A simple rule: small specific places = arrive AT, big places like cities/countries = arrive IN.

commonMistakes.wrong: He shouted on me for no reason.
commonMistakes.right: He shouted at me for no reason.
commonMistakes.why: When shouting is aimed angrily at a person, English uses 'shout at' ('shout to' is used only when calling out to someone far away in a friendly, neutral way). Since this sentence describes anger, 'at' is correct here.
'Tired of' must be followed by the -ing form of the verb, not the plain base form. Practice pairing 'tired of' directly with an -ing verb until it feels automatic.
'Fond' always pairs with 'of' to describe something you like. This is a simple, fixed phrase worth memorizing as one unit: fond OF.
The fixed legal and everyday expression is 'accused of' a crime or wrongdoing. Whenever discussing blame or accusation, default to 'of', not 'for'.
'Prevent' is followed by 'from' plus the -ing form, describing what action was stopped. Remember the complete structure: prevent someone FROM doing something.

'Talented' pairs with 'at' to show a specific skill, following the same pattern as 'good at' and 'skilled at'. Group these three words together in memory since they behave the same way: good/talented/skilled AT.

commonMistakes.wrong: I look forward to hear from you.
commonMistakes.right: I look forward to hearing from you.
commonMistakes.why: In the phrase 'look forward to', the word 'to' here is a preposition, so it must be followed by the -ing form, not the base verb. This is a very common mistake because 'to' usually signals an infinitive (to hear) elsewhere in English, but not in this specific phrase.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is famous by his voice.
commonMistakes.right: He is famous for his voice.
commonMistakes.why: 'Famous' pairs with 'for', showing the reason someone is well known. Practice the whole phrase: famous FOR + [reason].

commonMistakes.wrong: I am bored of this movie.
commonMistakes.right: I am bored with this movie.
commonMistakes.why: The more standard, traditional pairing is 'bored with', although 'bored of' has become common in casual speech. For formal writing and exams, choose 'bored with' to be safe.

commonMistakes.wrong: She was surprised of the news.
commonMistakes.right: She was surprised at the news.
commonMistakes.why: 'Surprised' pairs with 'at' (or sometimes 'by'), never 'of'. Whenever describing a reaction of surprise, reach for 'at' as your default choice.
'Scared' follows the same pattern as 'afraid' - both pair with 'of' to show the source of fear. Group 'afraid of' and 'scared of' together in your memory since they work identically.
'Participate' pairs with 'in', showing what activity someone took part in. Remember: you participate IN something, stepping into the activity.

'Believe' pairs with 'in' when expressing trust or confidence in a person or idea. This is different from simply 'believe someone' (meaning you accept what they say is true) versus 'believe in someone' (meaning you have faith in their ability).
As seen earlier, 'apologize' pairs with 'for' to state the reason for the apology. Keep the full structure in mind: apologize TO someone FOR something.
'Complain' pairs with 'about' to describe the subject of the complaint. This is a very common everyday phrase worth memorizing directly: complain ABOUT.
'Concentrate' pairs with 'on', showing where your focus and attention are directed. Picture your attention landing 'on' a target - that image can help this preposition stick in memory.

commonMistakes.wrong: She invested her money on stocks.
commonMistakes.right: She invested her money in stocks.
commonMistakes.why: 'Invest' pairs with 'in', showing where money is being placed. Remember: you invest IN something, putting your money into it.
'Specialize' pairs with 'in', describing a particular area of expertise. This follows the same pattern as 'interested in' and 'invest in' - many expertise-related verbs use 'in'.
'Rely' pairs with 'on', just like 'depend on' - the two words are near-synonyms and share the same preposition. If you remember 'depend on', you'll automatically remember 'rely on' too.
'Consist' pairs with 'of', describing what something is made up of. This is a simple fixed phrase: consist OF + [the parts].
Just like 'married to', the expression 'engaged to' describes a romantic commitment, and it never takes 'with'. Group 'engaged to' and 'married to' together since they share the same pattern.
'Subscribe' pairs with 'to', showing what service or publication you're signing up for. Remember the phrase as a whole: subscribe TO.
'Contribute' pairs with 'to', describing what something is added toward. This matches the pattern of many verbs showing direction or addition, like 'add to' and 'lead to'.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is qualified in this job.
commonMistakes.right: He is qualified for this job.
commonMistakes.why: 'Qualified' pairs with 'for', showing the role or position someone is suited for. Practice this as one phrase: qualified FOR a job/role.

commonMistakes.wrong: I dreamed with becoming a doctor.
commonMistakes.right: I dreamed of becoming a doctor.
commonMistakes.why: 'Dream' pairs with 'of' plus the -ing form when describing an ambition or wish. Remember the complete pattern: dream OF doing something.

commonMistakes.wrong: She was disappointed of the results.
commonMistakes.right: She was disappointed with the results.
commonMistakes.why: 'Disappointed' usually pairs with 'with' (or sometimes 'by'), but never 'of'. When talking about being let down by something, default to 'disappointed with'.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
options: I am good with English at.
quiz.explanation: 'Good' always pairs with 'at' when talking about a skill or subject.
'Discuss' already means 'talk about', so adding 'about' is unnecessary.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
quiz.explanation: The fixed expression is always 'married to'.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
quiz.explanation: 'Reach' already means 'arrive at', so no preposition is needed before the place.
